1:
"Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the first fruit of justification: peace with God. On "justified by faith," the grammatical voices note the ground. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having established justification by faith (chs. 3-4), now states its blessed fruits, the first being peace with God.

On "we have peace" (textual variant), the witnesses note the reading. Robertson notes the manuscripts divide between echomen ("we have peace," indicative) and echōmen ("let us have peace," hortatory subjunctive); most editors prefer the subjunctive on manuscript grounds, but the indicative ("we have") suits the doctrinal context and is widely defended.

On "peace with God," the puritan Henry notes it is more than the cessation of enmity — friendship and loving-kindness; the guilt that bred the quarrel removed, the sinner now at peace with God through Christ the peacemaker. The verse displays the first fruit: peace with God through Christ.

The witnesses agree: being justified by faith (the ground established in chs. 3-4, Barnes, Robertson), we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ — the first blessed fruit of justification. On the text they note the division: echomen ("we have peace," indicative) vs echōmen ("let us have peace," subjunctive) — most editors preferring the subjunctive on manuscript grounds, but the indicative suiting the doctrinal context (Robertson). Henry notes peace is more than the end of enmity — friendship and loving-kindness, the guilt removed, the sinner at peace through Christ the peacemaker. The verse displays the first fruit: peace with God. The verse marks the ground: justified by faith. The verse displays the textual question: we have / let us have. The verse displays the fruit: peace with God. The verse displays the means: through Christ. The first fruit displays the reconciliation that justification brings. The verse displays the peace: enmity ended. The verse displays the friendship: with God. The verse displays the mediator: Christ the peacemaker. Peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ displays the first and chief fruit of justification: the sinner, once at enmity, now reconciled and at peace with God — not merely the cessation of hostility but friendship and loving-kindness, secured through Christ the peacemaker. The verse displays the blessed consequence of justification by faith and the foundation of the believer's standing, and opens the catalogue of the believer's privileges (vv.2-11).
2:
"By whom also we have access by faith into this grace wherein we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God." The witnesses note the further fruits: access, standing, and hope. On "access... into this grace," the grammatical voices note the introduction. Barnes and Robertson note "access" (prosagoge) means introduction or admission — through Christ we have been brought into the state of grace (favour) in which we now stand.

On "wherein we stand," the witnesses note the standing. The believer stands firm in this grace — a settled, secure position of favour with God.

On "rejoice in hope of the glory of God," the witnesses note the hope. The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that justification brings not only present peace and standing but the joyful hope of future glory — the believer rejoicing in the prospect of sharing God's glory. The verse displays the fruits: access, standing in grace, and rejoicing hope of glory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: through Christ we have access (prosagoge, introduction, admission) by faith into this grace (favour) wherein we stand — a settled, secure position of favour (Barnes, Robertson) — and rejoice in hope of the glory of God, the joyful prospect of sharing God's glory (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the further fruits of justification. The verse marks the access: introduction into grace. The verse displays the standing: firm in favour. The verse displays the hope: rejoicing in glory. The verse displays the prospect: sharing God's glory. The further fruits display the believer's secure standing and joyful hope. The verse displays the admission: brought into grace. The verse displays the security: standing firm. The verse displays the rejoicing: in hope of glory. The further fruits of justification — access into grace, a firm standing in favour, and rejoicing in hope of the glory of God — display the believer's secure position and joyful prospect: through Christ admitted into the state of grace, standing firm in God's favour, and exulting in the hope of future glory. The verse displays the rich blessings flowing from justification by faith, and sets up the believer's joy even in tribulation (vv.3-5).
3:
"And not only so, but we glory in tribulations also: knowing that tribulation worketh patience;" The witnesses note glorying even in tribulations. On "we glory in tribulations," the grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note the believer rejoices (glories, exults) not only in hope but even in tribulations (afflictions) — finding cause for joy in suffering itself.

On "tribulation worketh patience," the witnesses note the reason. The ground of this glorying is knowing that tribulation produces patience (hupomone, endurance, steadfastness) — affliction, sanctified, develops Christian character.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the chain: tribulation, far from destroying faith, strengthens it, working patience; the believer glories because affliction yields spiritual fruit. The verse displays glorying in tribulations, which work patience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer glories (rejoices, exults) not only in hope but even in tribulations (Barnes, Robertson) — finding joy in suffering itself — knowing that tribulation works patience (hupomone, endurance, steadfastness), affliction sanctified developing Christian character. Calvin and Theodoret note tribulation, far from destroying faith, strengthens it; the believer glories because affliction yields spiritual fruit. The verse displays glorying in tribulations. The verse marks the paradox: glory in afflictions. The verse displays the reason: tribulation works patience. The verse displays the fruit: endurance developed. The verse displays the chain: suffering yielding character. Glorying in tribulations displays the transforming power of justifying grace. The verse displays the joy: even in suffering. The verse displays the product: patience. The verse displays the value: affliction sanctified. Glorying in tribulations, which work patience, displays the transforming power of justifying grace: the believer rejoices even in afflictions, knowing that suffering, sanctified by grace, produces endurance and develops Christian character. The verse displays the paradoxical joy of the justified, who find in tribulation not destruction but spiritual fruit, and begins the chain of grace's work in affliction (vv.4-5). The believer thus learns to read affliction not as the frown of God but as the instrument of grace, by which the Father trains his children and ripens in them the very graces that adorn the Christian life and prepare it for glory.
4:
"And patience, experience; and experience, hope:" The brief verse continues the chain of grace's work. On "patience, experience," the grammatical voices note the progression. Barnes and Robertson note "experience" (dokime) means proof, tried character, approvedness — patience (endurance) produces tested, proven character, as metal proved by fire.

On "experience, hope," the witnesses note the issue. The tried, approved character in turn produces hope — the experience of God's sustaining grace in trial strengthens the believer's hope of glory.

The Reformed Calvin notes the ascending chain: tribulation, patience, proof, hope — each step the fruit of grace, affliction ultimately strengthening the hope that justification gives. The verse displays the chain: patience yielding proven character, character yielding hope.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chain continues — patience (endurance) works experience (dokime, proof, tried and approved character, as metal proved by fire, Barnes, Robertson), and experience works hope (the tested character strengthening the believer's hope of glory). Calvin notes the ascending chain: tribulation, patience, proof, hope — each step the fruit of grace, affliction strengthening hope. The verse displays the chain. The verse marks the progression: patience to experience. The verse displays the proof: tried character. The verse displays the issue: hope. The verse displays the ascent: affliction to hope. The chain displays the fruitful work of grace in affliction. The verse displays the proving: character tested. The verse displays the strengthening: hope deepened. The verse displays the progression: each step from the last. The chain of patience yielding proven character, and character yielding hope, displays the fruitful work of grace in affliction: endurance produces tested, approved character, and that tried character in turn strengthens the believer's hope of glory. The verse displays the beautiful progression by which tribulation, sanctified, deepens rather than destroys the believer's hope, and leads to the assurance that this hope does not disappoint (v.5). Each link in this golden chain is forged by grace, so that the believer who begins in tribulation is carried forward, step by step, to a hope that rests secure and cannot finally be disappointed. What sin and Satan mean for the believer’s ruin, grace overrules for his good, turning the furnace of trial into the very means by which assurance is refined and deepened.
5:
"And hope maketh not ashamed; because the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us." The witnesses note the hope that does not disappoint, grounded in God's love. On "hope maketh not ashamed," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note this hope does not disappoint or put to shame — it will not fail, unlike worldly hopes; it is sure.

On "the love of God shed abroad in our hearts," the witnesses note the ground. Robertson and Calvin note "the love of God" here means God's love to us, poured out (shed abroad, lavishly diffused) in our hearts — the inward assurance of God's love confirming the hope. (Some take it as our love to God, but most as his love to us.)

On "by the Holy Ghost," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the agent: the Holy Spirit, given to believers, sheds abroad this sense of God's love — the Spirit's witness the ground of assured hope. The verse displays the hope that does not disappoint, secured by God's love poured out by the Spirit.

The witnesses agree: this hope does not disappoint or put to shame (it will not fail, unlike worldly hopes, Barnes, Robertson), because the love of God (his love to us, most hold) is shed abroad (poured out, lavishly diffused) in our hearts by the Holy Ghost given to us (Robertson, Calvin) — the Spirit's inward witness of God's love confirming the hope (Cyril). The verse displays the hope that does not disappoint. The verse marks the assurance: hope makes not ashamed. The verse displays the ground: God's love shed abroad. The verse displays the agent: the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the witness: the Spirit confirming love. The hope that does not disappoint displays the secure foundation of the believer's confidence. The verse displays the certainty: hope will not fail. The verse displays the love: poured out within. The verse displays the Spirit: the inward witness. The hope that does not disappoint, grounded in the love of God shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost, displays the secure foundation of the believer's confidence: unlike worldly hopes that fail, this hope is sure, sealed by the inward witness of the Spirit pouring out the sense of God's love. The verse displays the Holy Spirit's role in assurance, the experiential ground of the believer's unfailing hope, and turns to the supreme proof of God's love: the death of Christ for the ungodly (vv.6-8).
6:
"For when we were yet without strength, in due time Christ died for the ungodly." The witnesses note the supreme proof of God's love: Christ died for the ungodly. On "yet without strength," the grammatical voices note our condition. Barnes and Robertson note "without strength" (asthenes) means helpless, powerless — unable to save ourselves, morally weak and lost.

On "in due time," the witnesses note the timing. Christ died at the appointed time (the fullness of time) — the divinely fixed moment in God's plan.

On "Christ died for the ungodly," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the wonder: Christ died not for the righteous but for the ungodly (the impious) — the supreme demonstration of love, dying for those who had no claim, no strength, no merit. The verse displays the supreme proof: Christ died for the helpless ungodly at the appointed time.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when we were yet without strength (asthenes, helpless, powerless to save ourselves, Barnes, Robertson), in due time (the appointed, fixed moment in God's plan) Christ died for the ungodly (the impious) — the supreme demonstration of love, dying for those who had no claim, no strength, no merit (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays the supreme proof of God's love. The verse marks the condition: without strength. The verse displays the timing: in due time. The verse displays the wonder: Christ died for the ungodly. The verse displays the love: for the undeserving. The supreme proof displays the magnitude of God's love in Christ's death. The verse displays the helplessness: powerless to save ourselves. The verse displays the appointed time: God's plan. The verse displays the object: the ungodly. That when we were yet without strength Christ died for the ungodly displays the supreme proof of God's love: at the appointed time, Christ died not for the righteous or the deserving but for the helpless, impious sinner who had no claim and no power to save himself. The verse displays the astonishing magnitude of divine love, dying for the undeserving, and continues the demonstration of that love's surpassing greatness (vv.7-8).
7:
"For scarcely for a righteous man will one die: yet peradventure for a good man some would even dare to die." The witnesses note the contrast with human love. On "scarcely for a righteous man," the grammatical voices note the rarity. Barnes and Robertson note that among men, hardly anyone would die even for a righteous (just) man — such self-sacrifice is rare.

On "for a good man some would even dare to die," the witnesses note the slight distinction. Perhaps for a good man (benevolent, kind, lovable) someone might possibly dare to die — a higher love than for the merely righteous, yet still rare and only for the deserving.

The Reformed Calvin and others note the point: the contrast magnifies God's love (v.8) — even the rare heights of human love are only for the good or righteous, but Christ died for sinners. The verse displays the contrast: human love at its rare height is only for the deserving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: among men, hardly anyone would die even for a righteous (just) man (such self-sacrifice rare, Barnes, Robertson); perhaps for a good man (benevolent, lovable) someone might dare to die — a higher love, yet still rare and only for the deserving. Calvin notes the contrast magnifies God's love (v.8): even the rare heights of human love are only for the good, but Christ died for sinners. The verse displays the contrast with human love. The verse marks the rarity: scarcely for a righteous man. The verse displays the distinction: a good man might draw it. The verse displays the limit: only for the deserving. The verse displays the purpose: magnifying God's love. The contrast displays the surpassing greatness of God's love. The verse displays the rarity: human self-sacrifice scarce. The verse displays the heights: only for the good. The verse displays the foil: setting off divine love. The contrast with human love — that one would scarcely die for a righteous man, though perhaps for a good man some would dare — displays the surpassing greatness of God's love by comparison: even the rarest heights of human self-sacrifice are reached only for the deserving, but Christ, as the next verse shows, died for sinners. The verse displays the foil against which the magnitude of divine love shines, and leads to its climactic statement (v.8).
8:
"But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us." The witnesses note the climactic proof of God's love. On "God commendeth his love," the grammatical voices note the demonstration. Barnes and Robertson note "commendeth" (sunistesin) means proves, establishes, demonstrates — God sets forth, exhibits his love in a way beyond all human love.

On "while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us," the witnesses note the wonder. Unlike human love (which reaches only the good, v.7), God's love is shown in that Christ died for us while we were still sinners (his enemies, the ungodly) — not waiting for us to become worthy, but dying for the unworthy.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the surpassing greatness: this is love beyond compare — dying for sinners, not the righteous; the proof and ground of all the believer's assurance. The verse displays God's love commended in Christ's death for sinners.

The witnesses agree: God commends (sunistesin, proves, demonstrates, exhibits, Barnes, Robertson) his love toward us in that, while we were yet sinners (his enemies, the ungodly, not waiting for us to become worthy), Christ died for us — love beyond all human love (which reaches only the good, v.7). Calvin and Theodoret note the surpassing greatness: love beyond compare, dying for sinners not the righteous, the ground of all the believer's assurance. The verse displays God's love commended in Christ's death. The verse marks the demonstration: God commends his love. The verse displays the wonder: while yet sinners. The verse displays the death: Christ died for us. The verse displays the contrast: beyond human love. God's love commended displays the climactic proof of divine love. The verse displays the exhibition: love demonstrated. The verse displays the unworthiness: yet sinners. The verse displays the surpassing greatness: beyond compare. God commending his love in that Christ died for us while we were yet sinners displays the climactic and surpassing proof of divine love: unlike human love, which reaches only the deserving, God's love is shown in Christ's dying for the unworthy, the sinful, the ungodly — not waiting for merit but loving the unlovely. The verse displays the heart of the gospel and the unshakable ground of the believer's assurance, and sets up the certainty of full salvation (vv.9-11).
9:
"Much more then, being now justified by his blood, we shall be saved from wrath through him." The witnesses note the argument from the greater to the lesser: certainty of final salvation. On "much more," the grammatical voices note the a fortiori argument. Barnes and Robertson note Paul reasons from the greater to the lesser: if God did the harder thing (justifying us by Christ's blood while sinners), much more will he do the easier (save us from wrath).

On "justified by his blood," the witnesses note the ground. We are justified by Christ's blood (his atoning death) — the price of our justification.

On "saved from wrath through him," the witnesses note the certainty. Since we are already justified, we shall certainly be saved from the wrath to come (the final judgment) through Christ — the future salvation as sure as the present justification. The verse displays the certainty: justified by blood, much more saved from wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reasons a fortiori (from greater to lesser, Barnes, Robertson) — if God did the harder thing (justifying us by Christ's blood while sinners), much more will he do the easier (save us from wrath); being now justified by his blood (his atoning death, the price of justification), we shall be saved from wrath (the final judgment) through him — the future salvation as sure as the present justification. The verse displays the certainty of final salvation. The verse marks the argument: much more, from greater to lesser. The verse displays the ground: justified by his blood. The verse displays the assurance: saved from wrath. The verse displays the security: future as sure as present. The certainty displays the unshakable assurance of the justified. The verse displays the a fortiori reasoning: the harder done, the easier sure. The verse displays the blood: the price paid. The verse displays the deliverance: from wrath to come. The certainty of final salvation — being justified by his blood, much more shall we be saved from wrath through him — displays the unshakable assurance of the justified: since God has already done the greater thing in justifying us by Christ's blood while we were sinners, he will certainly do the lesser in saving us from the wrath to come. The verse displays the a fortiori ground of the believer's confidence, the future salvation as sure as the present justification, and continues the argument from reconciliation (v.10).
10:
"For if, when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by his life." The witnesses note the further a fortiori argument from reconciliation. On "when we were enemies, reconciled by the death of his Son," the grammatical voices note the greater work. Barnes and Robertson note that while we were enemies (at enmity with God), we were reconciled by Christ's death — the harder, costlier work, accomplished for the hostile.

On "much more... saved by his life," the witnesses note the lesser, certain work. If God reconciled us while enemies by Christ's death, much more, now reconciled (his friends), shall we be saved by his life — Christ's risen, living intercession securing our final salvation.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the argument: the death reconciled, the life saves; if the costlier was done for enemies, the easier is sure for friends. The verse displays the certainty: reconciled by his death, saved by his life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if, while we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son (the harder, costlier work, for the hostile, Barnes, Robertson), much more, being now reconciled (his friends), shall we be saved by his life — Christ's risen, living intercession securing our final salvation. Calvin and Theodoret note the argument: the death reconciled, the life saves; the costlier done for enemies, the easier sure for friends. The verse displays the certainty from reconciliation. The verse marks the greater work: reconciled while enemies. The verse displays the means: the death of his Son. The verse displays the lesser work: saved by his life. The verse displays the assurance: certain for friends. The certainty displays the assurance grounded in Christ's death and life. The verse displays the reconciliation: enemies made friends. The verse displays the death: the costly price. The verse displays the life: the risen Christ's intercession. The certainty of salvation — reconciled to God by the death of his Son while enemies, much more saved by his life now reconciled — displays the believer's assurance grounded in both Christ's death and his living intercession: if God did the costlier work of reconciling enemies through Christ's death, much more will he complete the easier work of saving his friends through Christ's risen life. The verse displays the two-fold ground of assurance in the dying and living Christ, and leads to the believer's joy in God (v.11).
11:
"And not only so, but we also joy in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom we have now received the atonement." The witnesses note the crowning fruit: joy in God. On "joy in God," the grammatical voices note the climax. Barnes and Robertson note the believer not only is justified, has peace, and is assured of salvation, but joys (rejoices, exults) in God himself — the highest blessing, delight in God.

On "through our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the means. This joy is through Christ, the mediator — all the believer's relation to God flows through him.

On "received the atonement," the witnesses note the ground. The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note "atonement" (katallage) means reconciliation — we have now received reconciliation through Christ, the ground of our joy in God. The verse displays the crowning fruit: joy in God through Christ, by whom we received reconciliation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer not only is justified, has peace, and is assured of salvation, but joys (rejoices, exults) in God himself — the highest blessing, delight in God (Barnes, Robertson) — through our Lord Jesus Christ (the mediator), by whom we have now received the atonement (katallage, reconciliation, the ground of our joy, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the crowning fruit: joy in God. The verse marks the climax: joy in God himself. The verse displays the means: through Christ. The verse displays the ground: received reconciliation. The verse displays the delight: in God. The crowning fruit displays the believer's highest privilege: joy in God. The verse displays the exultation: rejoicing in God. The verse displays the mediator: through Christ. The verse displays the reconciliation: now received. The crowning fruit of justification — joy in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom we have received the reconciliation — displays the believer's highest privilege: not merely peace, standing, and assurance, but delight in God himself, the deepest joy, grounded in the reconciliation accomplished through Christ. The verse displays the climax of the catalogue of the justified believer's blessings, and closes the first part of the chapter before the great Adam-Christ comparison (vv.12-21).
12:
"Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin; and so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned:" The witnesses note the great Adam-Christ parallel and the original-sin crux. On "by one man sin entered... death by sin," the grammatical voices note the historical fact. Barnes, Robertson, and Theodoret note that through Adam's one transgression sin entered the world, and death (its penalty) by sin — death then spreading to all.

On "death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned" (eph ho pantes hemarton), the witnesses divide sharply. The Reformed Calvin and the Augustinian tradition (with the Vulgate, Beza) take it as "in whom [Adam] all sinned" — all sinned in Adam, their representative and natural head, inheriting his guilt and corruption (original sin); Calvin: we are all imbued with natural depravity from the womb, sin before God. Barnes and Robertson take eph ho as "because all sinned" — death passed on all because all in fact sin personally (Barnes denying imputation of Adam's sin, Robertson noting the constative aorist gathering the race's history).

On Adam as type, the eastern Chrysostom and Theodoret note Adam's fall made all mortal; the parallel with Christ is set up. The verse displays the entrance of sin and death through Adam and the original-sin crux.

The witnesses agree the verse opens the Adam-Christ parallel: through Adam's one sin, sin entered the world and death by sin, death passing on all — but divide sharply on "eph ho pantes hemarton." The Reformed Calvin (with Augustine, the Vulgate, Beza) reads "in whom all sinned": all sinned in Adam their head, inheriting his guilt and the corruption of nature (original sin), sin before God from the womb. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read "because all sinned": death passed on all because all in fact sin personally (Barnes denying imputation of Adam's sin; Robertson noting the constative aorist gathering the race's sin). Chrysostom and Theodoret note Adam's fall made all mortal. The verse displays the entrance of sin and death and the original-sin crux. The verse marks the fact: sin entered by one man. The verse displays the penalty: death by sin. The verse displays the crux: in whom / because all sinned. The verse displays the parallel: Adam and Christ. The verse displays the foundation of the comparison. The entrance of sin and death through Adam displays the ruin the second Adam came to repair. The verse displays the spread: death to all. The verse displays the debate: original sin. The verse displays the type: Adam figuring Christ. The entrance of sin and death through one man, and the crux "for that all have sinned," displays the foundation of the great Adam-Christ parallel and the deepest question of original sin: whether all sinned in Adam (the Reformed/Augustinian view of inherited guilt and corruption) or death passed on all because all personally sin. The verse displays the ruin introduced by the first Adam, the dark backdrop against which the abounding grace of the second Adam will shine, and opens the climactic comparison (vv.13-21).
13:
"(For until the law sin was in the world: but sin is not imputed when there is no law." The witnesses note the proof that sin reigned before the law. On "until the law sin was in the world," the grammatical voices note the fact. Barnes and Robertson note that from Adam to Moses (before the law was given) sin existed in the world — men sinned, and died.

On "sin is not imputed when there is no law," the witnesses note the principle. Sin is not reckoned (charged formally) where there is no explicit law — yet men died in that period, proving sin and its penalty were present even without the written law.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the argument: the reign of death before the law proves that sin (the sin of Adam, and men's own) was in the world; the law made sin more known but did not introduce it. The verse displays the proof: sin reigned before the law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: from Adam to Moses (before the law was given) sin was in the world — men sinned and died (Barnes, Robertson); and though sin is not imputed (formally charged) where there is no explicit law, men nevertheless died in that period, proving sin and its penalty were present even without the written law. Calvin and Theodoret note the reign of death before the law proves sin was in the world; the law made sin more known but did not introduce it. The verse displays the proof that sin reigned before the law. The verse marks the fact: sin before the law. The verse displays the principle: sin not formally imputed without law. The verse displays the evidence: death reigning. The verse displays the conclusion: sin present without the law. The proof displays the universality of sin and death from Adam. The verse displays the period: Adam to Moses. The verse displays the death: reigning before the law. The verse displays the argument: sin present without the law. The proof that sin reigned before the law — sin in the world from Adam to Moses, men dying though sin was not formally imputed without explicit law — displays the universality of sin and death traceable to Adam: the reign of death in that lawless period proves that sin (Adam's and men's own) was present, the law afterward making it known but not creating it. The verse displays the evidence for the universal effect of Adam's sin, and continues to the reign of death (v.14).
14:
"Nevertheless death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over them that had not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression, who is the figure of him that was to come." The witnesses note death's reign and Adam as the type of Christ. On "death reigned from Adam to Moses," the grammatical voices note the evidence. Barnes and Robertson note that death reigned (held universal sway) even before the law — proving sin's presence and the effect of Adam's transgression.

On "them that had not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression," the witnesses note the difficulty. Many (including infants, and those without an explicit command like Adam's) died though they had not sinned exactly as Adam did (against a positive law) — yet death reigned over them, pointing to their share in Adam's sin.

On "the figure of him that was to come," the eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note Adam is the type (figure, tupos) of Christ — the one whose act affected the many, paralleling Christ. The verse displays death's reign and Adam as the figure of Christ.

The witnesses agree: death reigned (held universal sway) from Adam to Moses, even over those who had not sinned exactly as Adam did (against a positive command) — including infants — proving their share in Adam's sin and the effect of his transgression (Barnes, Robertson); and Adam is the figure (tupos, type) of him that was to come, Christ — the one whose act affected the many, paralleling Christ (Theodoret, Calvin). The verse displays death's reign and Adam as the type of Christ. The verse marks the evidence: death reigned. The verse displays the difficulty: those who sinned not as Adam. The verse displays the share: in Adam's sin. The verse displays the type: Adam figuring Christ. Death's reign displays the universal effect of Adam's transgression. The verse displays the sway: death over all. The verse displays the infants: dying though without Adam's law. The verse displays the type: Adam and Christ. Death's reign from Adam to Moses, even over those who had not sinned as Adam did, and Adam as the figure of him that was to come, displays both the universal effect of Adam's transgression (death reigning even over infants, pointing to their share in his sin) and the structure of the great parallel: Adam the type of Christ, the one whose act determines the destiny of the many. The verse displays the head-and-representative principle that governs the comparison, and turns to the contrast between the offence and the free gift (vv.15-17).
15:
"But not as the offence, so also is the free gift. For if through the offence of one many be dead, much more the grace of God, and the gift by grace, which is by one man, Jesus Christ, hath abounded unto many." The witnesses note the dissimilarity — the free gift exceeds the offence. Gill notes by "the offence" is meant Adam's first sin, by which he fell and all his posterity with him; by "the free gift" the righteousness of Christ which justifies; both Adam and Christ are "one man," for the comparison's sake. The witnesses note Paul drawing the contrast — the free gift is not merely like the offence, but far exceeds it; if by one man's offence many died, much more has the grace of God and the gift by Christ abounded to many; the superabounding of grace over the ruin, the antitype surpassing the type. The witnesses note the free gift and the offence. The witnesses note grace abounding much more.

The witnesses note the free gift far exceeding the offence — grace abounding much more than the ruin; the antitype surpassing the type.

So the witnesses make the verse the superabounding of grace over the offence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the free gift is not merely like the offence, but far exceeds it — grace abounding much more than the ruin. The witnesses note the dissimilarity: "not as the offence, so also is the free gift" — Gill notes by "the offence" is meant Adam's first sin, by "the free gift" the righteousness of Christ; the gift not merely parallel to the offence, but exceeding it; the dissimilarity; the gift greater. The expositors observe the "much more": "if through the offence of one many be dead, much more the grace of God... hath abounded unto many" — if Adam's one offence brought death on many, much more has the grace of God abounded to many; the "much more"; the grace exceeding the offence; the gift surpassing the ruin. The witnesses press the superabounding of grace: the grace of God and the gift by Christ have "abounded" (superabounded) unto many — far exceeding the effect of the offence; the grace overflowing; the gift more than restoring; the abundance of grace; the superabounding mercy. The expositors draw the lesson on the antitype surpassing the type: Adam is the figure of Christ (v.14), but the antitype surpasses the type — the grace exceeds the ruin, the gift the offence, the life the death; the type surpassed; the second Adam greater than the first; the grace abounding over the sin (v.20). The witnesses observe the two heads: "which is by one man, Jesus Christ" — as the offence was by one man (Adam), the gift is by one man (Jesus Christ); the two heads; the one and the one; Adam and Christ; the parallel of the heads, with the gift exceeding. The expositors note the comfort of the superabounding: the superabounding of grace is the believer's comfort — for the gift more than repairs the ruin; the grace that abounds beyond the fall; the more-than-restoration; the comfort of the abounding grace; the gospel's triumph over the fall. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the free gift exceeding the offence, the grace abounding much more, and the antitype surpassing the type. The verse thus records the free gift far exceeding the offence — teaching the superabounding of grace over the ruin, the gift by Christ surpassing the offence by Adam.
16:
"And not as it was by one that sinned, so is the gift: for the judgment was by one to condemnation, but the free gift is of many offences unto justification." The witnesses note a second dissimilarity — the gift covers many offences. Barnes notes this is the second point of difference: the evil consequences flowed from one crime, one act of guilt (Adam's); but the favours had respect to many acts of guilt — the gift covering many offences. The witnesses note Paul drawing the further contrast — the judgment came by one offence unto condemnation, but the free gift covers many offences unto justification; the grace not only exceeding but reaching further, justifying from the whole multitude of sins, not Adam's one only; the abundance of the gift in the breadth of its pardon. The witnesses note one offence to condemnation. The witnesses note the gift of many offences to justification.

The witnesses note a second contrast — the judgment from one offence to condemnation, the gift covering many offences to justification.

So the witnesses make the verse the second dissimilarity — the gift covers many offences. The lesson the expositors draw is that the grace not only exceeds but reaches further, justifying from the whole multitude of sins. The witnesses note the contrast: "not as it was by one that sinned, so is the gift" — Barnes notes this is the second point of difference; the gift not merely parallel to the condemnation; the further dissimilarity; the gift greater in its reach. The expositors observe the judgment from one: "the judgment was by one to condemnation" — the judgment (condemnation) came by one offence (Adam's one sin) unto condemnation; the one offence; the single sin bringing the condemnation; the judgment from the one. The witnesses press the gift of many offences: "the free gift is of many offences unto justification" — the free gift covers many offences unto justification; Barnes notes the favours had respect to many acts of guilt; the gift reaching the many sins; the justification from the multitude; the pardon of many offences. The expositors draw the lesson on the breadth of the gift: the condemnation flowed from one offence; the justification covers many offences — Adam's one sin and all the believer's actual sins; the gift reaching further than the offence; the breadth of the pardon; the justification from all sins; the grace covering the multitude. The witnesses observe the abundance in the reach: the gift is abundant not only in exceeding the offence (v.15), but in its breadth — justifying from many offences; the abundance in the reach; the grace that covers all sins; the pardon comprehensive; the gift far-reaching. The expositors note the comfort of the comprehensive pardon: the gift justifies from many offences — the believer pardoned not only of the imputed sin of Adam, but of all his own sins; the comprehensive pardon; the comfort of the full justification; the many sins covered; the complete acquittal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the one offence to condemnation, the gift of many offences to justification, and the breadth of the pardon. The verse thus records the second contrast — the gift covering many offences unto justification — teaching that the grace reaches further than the offence, justifying from the whole multitude of sins.
17:
"For if by one man's offence death reigned by one; much more they which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ.)" The witnesses note the reign in life through Christ. Gill notes it may be rendered "by one offence death reigned by one," for it was the single sin of Adam that gave death its reigning power over men. Barnes notes the same idea condensed — under the administration of a just and merciful God, by the offence of one, death has reigned. The witnesses note Paul crowning the contrast — if by one man's offence death reigned, much more shall those who receive the abundance of grace and the gift of righteousness reign in life by Christ; the believers not merely delivered from death's reign, but made to reign in life — a fuller triumph than the ruin, kings in life through the one Saviour. The witnesses note death reigned by one. The witnesses note believers reign in life.

The witnesses note believers reigning in life through Christ — a fuller triumph than the ruin; kings in life, not merely freed from death.

So the witnesses make the verse the reign in life through Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers, receiving abundant grace, are made not merely free from death's reign but to reign in life — a fuller triumph than the ruin. The witnesses note the reign of death: "if by one man's offence death reigned by one" — Gill notes it was the single sin of Adam that gave death its reigning power over men; death reigned by one (Adam's one offence); the reign of death; the dominion through the one sin. The expositors observe the "much more": "much more they which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ" — much more shall those who receive the abundance of grace reign in life by Christ; the "much more"; the greater triumph; the abundance of grace. The witnesses press the reign in life: the believers shall "reign in life" — not merely be delivered from death's reign, but themselves reign in life; the fuller triumph; the believers made kings; the reigning in life; the triumph exceeding the ruin; "they shall reign for ever and ever" (Revelation 22:5). The expositors draw the lesson on the greater triumph: under Adam, death reigned over men; under Christ, the believers reign in life — the triumph exceeding the ruin; not merely the reversal of death's reign, but the believers reigning; the more-than-restoration; the fuller victory; the grace abounding over the fall. The witnesses observe the abundance of grace and the gift of righteousness: the believers "receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness" — the abundant grace and the imputed righteousness received; the gift received by faith; the abundance; the righteousness of Christ the gift; the grace and the righteousness. The expositors note the reigning through Christ: the believers reign in life "by one, Jesus Christ" — through the one Saviour; the reign through Christ; the kings made by him; the triumph through the second Adam; the life and the reign by Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the death reigned by one, the believers reign in life, and the fuller triumph through Christ. The verse thus records the believers reigning in life through Christ — teaching that the abundant grace makes the believers not merely free from death but to reign in life, a fuller triumph than the ruin.
18:
"Therefore as by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation; even so by the righteousness of one the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life." The witnesses note the summing-up parallel of Adam and Christ. Barnes notes this is the summing up of what had been stated, resuming the proposition of v.12; as by one offence judgment came on all to condemnation, so by one righteousness the free gift came on all unto justification of life. The witnesses note Paul gathering the parallel — as by Adam's one offence condemnation came on all men, so by Christ's one righteousness the free gift comes on all men unto justification of life; the two heads and their two effects; the "all" in Adam and the "all" in Christ — the latter, the witnesses hold, all who are in Christ, the believing, as the former all who are in Adam. The witnesses note condemnation by one offence. The witnesses note justification by one righteousness.

The witnesses note the parallel — condemnation on all by Adam's offence, justification of life by Christ's righteousness; the two heads.

So the witnesses make the verse the summing-up parallel of Adam and Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the parallel of the two heads — condemnation on all in Adam, justification of life on all in Christ. The witnesses note the condemnation: "as by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation" — by Adam's one offence, judgment (condemnation) came on all men; the condemnation by the one offence; the all in Adam condemned; the ruin through the one. The expositors observe the parallel justification: "even so by the righteousness of one the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life" — by Christ's one righteousness, the free gift came on all men unto justification of life; the justification by the one righteousness; the gift of life; the parallel; Barnes notes the summing up of v.12. The witnesses weigh the two "all": the witnesses hold the parallel — the "all" in Adam (all who are in him by nature, the whole race) condemned; the "all" in Christ (all who are in him by faith, the believing) justified; the "all" defined by the head; not that all without exception are justified (universalism), but all who are in Christ, as all who are in Adam; the two "all" parallel in structure, each defined by its head. The expositors draw the lesson on the two heads and their effects: Adam and Christ are the two federal heads — Adam's offence bringing condemnation on all his, Christ's righteousness bringing justification on all his; the two heads; the two humanities; the ruin and the redemption; the parallel of the heads. The witnesses present the debate on the "all": most (Reformed) hold the second "all" means all in Christ (the elect, the believing), parallel to all in Adam; some emphasize the universal sufficiency of the provision (the gift offered to all); but the justification of life is actually received only by those in Christ; the "all" defined by union with the head. The expositors note the "justification of life": the free gift is "unto justification of life" — the justification that issues in life; the acquittal bringing life; the justification and the life joined; the gift of life through the righteousness; the life-giving justification. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the condemnation by one offence, the justification by one righteousness, and the two heads. The verse thus records the parallel of Adam and Christ — condemnation on all in Adam, justification of life on all in Christ — teaching the two federal heads and their two effects.
19:
"For as by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous." The witnesses note the constituting of sinners and righteous by the two heads. Barnes notes this is not a mere repetition but an explanation — guarding against the inference that men were condemned without guilt; the apostle affirms they are in fact sinners; and (citing Calvin) lest any arrogate to himself innocence, Paul shows men were made sinners by Adam. The witnesses note Paul declaring that by Adam's one disobedience many were made (constituted) sinners, and by Christ's one obedience many shall be made (constituted) righteous; the imputation of Adam's disobedience constituting the race sinners, and the imputation of Christ's obedience constituting the believing righteous; the heart of the doctrine of imputation. The witnesses note made sinners by disobedience. The witnesses note made righteous by obedience.

The witnesses note many made sinners by Adam's disobedience, made righteous by Christ's obedience — the heart of imputation.

So the witnesses make the verse the constituting of sinners and righteous. The lesson the expositors draw is the imputation of Adam's disobedience constituting the race sinners, and the imputation of Christ's obedience constituting the believing righteous. The witnesses note the constituting of sinners: "by one man's disobedience many were made sinners" — by Adam's one disobedience, many were made (constituted, set down as) sinners; Barnes notes the apostle affirms they are in fact sinners; the many made sinners; the constituting by Adam's disobedience; the imputation of the sin. The expositors observe Barnes's guard, citing Calvin: lest any arrogate to himself innocence, Paul shows men were made sinners by Adam — not condemned without guilt, but constituted sinners by the disobedience of the head; the guilt real; the sinners constituted; the imputation guarded. The witnesses press the constituting of righteous: "so by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous" — by Christ's one obedience, many shall be made (constituted, set down as) righteous; the many made righteous; the constituting by Christ's obedience; the imputation of the righteousness. The expositors draw the lesson on the parallel imputation: as Adam's disobedience was imputed, constituting the race sinners, so Christ's obedience is imputed, constituting the believing righteous; the parallel imputation; the sin reckoned, the righteousness reckoned; the two imputations; the heart of the doctrine. The witnesses observe "the obedience of one": the "obedience of one" (Christ) — his whole obedience, active and passive, his perfect law-keeping and his death; the obedience imputed; the righteousness of Christ; the obedience that constitutes the believer righteous; "by the obedience of one." The expositors note the "made" (constituted): "made sinners... made righteous" — the word means constituted, set down as, reckoned; not made inherently (by infusion), but constituted by imputation — the sinners by the imputed disobedience, the righteous by the imputed obedience; the constituting by reckoning; the imputation, not infusion; the forensic constituting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the made sinners by disobedience, the made righteous by obedience, and the parallel imputation. The verse thus records many made sinners by Adam's disobedience and righteous by Christ's obedience — teaching the heart of the doctrine of imputation, the two heads constituting their own.
20:
"Moreover the law entered, that the offence might abound. But where sin abounded, grace did much more abound:" The witnesses note the law entering and grace abounding over sin. Gill notes by "the law" is meant chiefly the moral law as it came by Moses, entering with great solemnity on Sinai, coming in between Adam's sin and Christ's sacrifice, over and above the promise; by which sin abounded and appeared very sinful. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the law entered that the offence might abound — revealing and increasing the knowledge and guilt of sin — but that where sin abounded, grace did much more abound; the law's purpose to make sin appear exceeding sinful, and the triumph of superabounding grace over the abounding sin. The witnesses note the law and abounding sin. The witnesses note grace much more abounding.

The witnesses note the law entering that sin might abound — but grace abounding much more; the triumph of grace over sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the law and the superabounding grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the law's purpose to make sin abound (appear exceeding sinful), and the triumph of superabounding grace over the abounding sin. The witnesses note the law's entry: "the law entered, that the offence might abound" — Gill notes by "the law" is meant chiefly the moral law as it came by Moses, entering with solemnity on Sinai, coming in between Adam's sin and Christ's sacrifice; the law entered; the law's coming; the law added. The expositors observe the purpose: "that the offence might abound" — the law entered that the offence might abound — revealing sin, increasing the knowledge and guilt of it, making it appear exceeding sinful (Romans 7:13); the law making sin abound; not causing sin, but revealing and aggravating it; the sin made manifest. The witnesses press the law's function: the law makes the offence abound — by defining sin, forbidding it (and so provoking the rebellious heart), and revealing its sinfulness; the law revealing the sin; the offence made to abound by the law's light; the sin exposed and aggravated; the law's convicting work. The expositors draw the lesson on the superabounding grace: "where sin abounded, grace did much more abound" — where sin abounded (through the law's revealing), grace did much more abound (superabound); the grace exceeding the sin; the triumph of grace over sin; the grace abounding beyond the abounding sin; the victory of grace. The witnesses observe the "much more abound": grace did "much more abound" (superabound, overflow) — exceeding the abounding sin; the grace overflowing; the mercy exceeding the sin; the grace that conquers the abounding sin; the superabounding grace. The expositors note the comfort and the guard: the superabounding grace is the believer's comfort — grace exceeds the sin; yet (Romans 6:1) this is no license to sin, for grace abounds to deliver from sin, not to indulge it; the comfort guarded; the grace that conquers sin; against the abuse of the doctrine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the law and abounding sin, the grace much more abounding, and the triumph of grace. The verse thus records the law entering that sin might abound, but grace abounding much more — teaching the law's purpose to make sin appear exceeding sinful, and the triumph of superabounding grace over sin.
21:
"That as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord." The witnesses note the reign of grace through righteousness unto eternal life. Gill notes this illustrates the grace of God by comparing the reigns of sin and grace: sin has a kingly, governing, commanding power, its dominion universal over men in nature; but grace reigns to a contrary end. The witnesses note Paul crowning the chapter — that as sin reigned unto death, even so grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Christ; the two reigns contrasted, sin reigning to death and grace reigning (through the imputed righteousness) to eternal life; the triumph of reigning grace, the final word of the great argument. The witnesses note sin reigned unto death. The witnesses note grace reigns unto eternal life.

The witnesses note grace reigning through righteousness unto eternal life — the two reigns contrasted, the triumph of reigning grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the reign of grace unto eternal life. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast of the two reigns — sin reigning unto death, grace reigning through righteousness unto eternal life. The witnesses note the reign of sin: "as sin hath reigned unto death" — Gill notes sin has a kingly, governing, commanding power, its dominion universal over men in nature; sin reigned unto death; the reign of sin; the dominion ending in death; the tyranny of sin. The expositors observe the reign of grace: "even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord" — grace reigns, through righteousness, unto eternal life, by Christ; the reign of grace; the contrasting kingdom; the grace enthroned; the reign to life. The witnesses press the contrast of the reigns: sin reigned unto death; grace reigns unto eternal life; the two reigns; the two kingdoms; the tyranny of sin and the reign of grace; the death and the life; the contrast. The expositors draw the lesson on grace reigning through righteousness: grace reigns "through righteousness" — not apart from righteousness, but through the imputed righteousness of Christ; the grace that reigns by the righteousness; the throne of grace established on the righteousness; the grace and the righteousness joined; the reign of grace grounded in the righteousness of Christ. The witnesses observe the end — eternal life: grace reigns unto "eternal life" — the end of the reign of grace; the life eternal; the consummation; the gift of grace; the eternal life by Christ; "the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord" (Romans 6:23). The expositors note the crowning of the argument: this verse crowns the great argument (Romans 3-5) — the triumph of reigning grace through Christ's righteousness unto eternal life; the final word; the consummation of the doctrine; the reign of grace the climax; the gospel's triumph. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sin reigned unto death, the grace reigns unto eternal life, and the triumph of reigning grace. The verse thus records grace reigning through righteousness unto eternal life by Christ — teaching the contrast of the two reigns and the triumph of reigning grace, the crown of the great argument.
